title: अवधूत गीता (प्रमुख श्लोक)
devanagariText:
॥ श्री अवधूत गीता (प्रमुख श्लोक) ॥

॥ अथ प्रथमोऽध्यायः ॥

ईश्वरानुग्रहादेव पुंसामद्वैतवासना।
महद्भयपरित्राणाद्विप्राणामुपजायते॥१॥

सकृद्विभातं चैतन्यमेतद्ध्यानान्न विद्यते।
न ध्यानं चैतन्यरूपं स्वयमेव प्रकाशते॥२॥

आकाशवत् सर्वगतोऽहमच्युतः
आदित्यवत् सर्वविकासकारणम्।
नित्यं शुद्धं निराभासं निर्विकल्पं निरञ्जनम्।
सच्चिदानन्दमद्वैतं तद्ब्रह्माहमिति स्मर॥३॥

कथं तु चित्तं विमलं कथं वा ये वा संसारे।
तत्त्वं ममैव सर्वत्र सदा नित्यं निरञ्जनम्॥४॥

न मे बन्धोऽस्ति मोक्षो वा भ्रान्तिः शान्ता निराश्रया।
अहो मयि स्थितं विश्वं वस्तुतो न मयि स्थितम्॥५॥

॥ अथ द्वितीयोऽध्यायः ॥

मनोवचोभिरव्यक्तं प्रकाशं सर्वरूपकम्।
नेत्रेन्द्रियैरपि प्राप्तं कथमत्रोपदिश्यते॥६॥

न वेदशास्त्रं न शास्ता न शिष्यो न च देवता।
अयं सदा पूर्णमात्मा स्वयं ज्योतिः प्रकाशते॥७॥

नाहं कर्ता नाहं भोक्ता न मे कर्म कदाचन।
न मे देहो न मे देही परं ब्रह्माहमेव तत्॥८॥

॥ अथ तृतीयोऽध्यायः ॥

सर्वं खल्विदं ब्रह्म नेह नानास्ति किञ्चन।
आत्मैवेदं सर्वमिति वक्ति वेदान्तडिण्डिमः॥९॥

पञ्चभूतात्मकं विश्वं मरीचिजलसन्निभम्।
कस्यापि कथमप्येतन्नमस्कुर्यात्कथं भवेत्॥१०॥

॥ अथ चतुर्थोऽध्यायः ॥

न ज्ञानं न च वा ज्ञेयं न ज्ञातृत्वं कदाचन।
ज्ञानघन एवायं परं ब्रह्मास्मि सर्वदा॥११॥

न मे रागो न मे द्वेषो न मे लोभो न मत्सरः।
धर्मार्थकाममोक्षेषु किं मे प्रयोजनम्॥१२॥

यत्र गच्छति तत्ब्रह्म यत्तिष्ठति तत्परम्।
यत्पश्यति तदद्वैतमवधूतो मनोगतम्॥१३॥

निर्गुणं निष्क्रियं शान्तं निरवद्यं निरञ्जनम्।
आनन्दमयमद्वन्द्वमवधूतं स्वरूपतः॥१४॥

सदैव मुक्तो निर्लेपो निःसङ्गो निर्मलोऽमलः।
एतन्मम स्वरूपं हि अवधूतं स्वयं शिवम्॥१५॥

॥ इति श्री अवधूत गीता प्रमुख श्लोक सम्पूर्णाः ॥
transliteration:
|| Shrī Avadhūta Gītā (Pramukha Śloka) ||

|| Atha Prathamo'dhyāyaḥ ||

Īśvarānugrahādeva puṁsāmadvaitavāsanā.
Mahadbhayaparitrāṇādviprāṇāmupajāyate.||1||

Sakṛdvibhātaṁ chaitanyametaddhyānānna vidyate.
Na dhyānaṁ chaitanyarūpaṁ svayameva prakāśate.||2||

Ākāśavat sarvagatohamachyutaḥ
Ādityavat sarvavikāsakāraṇam.
Nityaṁ śuddhaṁ nirābhāsaṁ nirvikalpaṁ nirañjanam.
Sachchidānandamadvaitaṁ tadbrahmāhamiti smara.||3||

Kathaṁ tu chittaṁ vimalaṁ kathaṁ vā ye vā saṁsāre.
Tattvaṁ mamaiva sarvatra sadā nityaṁ nirañjanam.||4||

Na me bandho'sti mokṣo vā bhrāntiḥ śāntā nirāśrayā.
Aho mayi sthitaṁ viśvaṁ vastuto na mayi sthitam.||5||

|| Atha Dvitīyo'dhyāyaḥ ||

Manovachobhiravyaktaṁ prakāśaṁ sarvarūpakam.
Netrendriyairapi prāptaṁ kathamatropadiśyate.||6||

Na Vedaśāstraṁ na śāstā na śiṣyo na cha devatā.
Ayaṁ sadā pūrṇamātmā svayaṁ jyotiḥ prakāśate.||7||

Nāhaṁ kartā nāhaṁ bhoktā na me karma kadāchana.
Na me deho na me dehī paraṁ brahmāhameva tat.||8||

|| Atha Tṛtīyo'dhyāyaḥ ||

Sarvaṁ khalvidaṁ brahma neha nānāsti kiñchana.
Ātmaividaṁ sarvamiti vakti vedāntaḍiṇḍimaḥ.||9||

Pañchabhūtātmakaṁ viśvaṁ marīchijalasannibham.
Kasyāpi kathamapyetannamaskuryāt kathaṁ bhavet.||10||

|| Atha Chaturtho'dhyāyaḥ ||

Na jñānaṁ na cha vā jñeyaṁ na jñātṛtvaṁ kadāchana.
Jñānaghana evāyaṁ paraṁ brahmāsmi sarvadā.||11||

Na me rāgo na me dveṣo na me lobho na matsaraḥ.
Dharmārthakāmamokṣeṣu kiṁ me prayojanam.||12||

Yatra gachchhati tadbrahma yattiṣṭhati tatparam.
Yatpaśyati tadadvaitamavadhūto manogatam.||13||

Nirguṇaṁ niṣkriyaṁ śāntaṁ niravadyaṁ nirañjanam.
Ānandamayamadvandvamavadhūtaṁ svarūpataḥ.||14||

Sadaiva mukto nirlepo niḥsaṅgo nirmalo'malaḥ.
Etanmama svarūpaṁ hi avadhūtaṁ svayaṁ śivam.||15||

|| Iti Shrī Avadhūta Gītā Pramukha Śloka sampūrṇāḥ ||
hindiMeaning: (1) ईश्वर की कृपा से ही मनुष्यों में अद्वैत भावना उत्पन्न होती है, जो महाभय से रक्षा करती है। (2) एक बार प्रकाशित चैतन्य ध्यान से नहीं प्राप्त होता; वह स्वयं प्रकाशित होता है। (3) आकाश की तरह सर्वव्यापी, अच्युत, सूर्य की तरह सब विकास का कारण हूँ। नित्य शुद्ध, निराभास, निर्विकल्प, निरंजन, सच्चिदानन्द अद्वैत - वह ब्रह्म मैं हूँ, ऐसा स्मरण करो। (5) न मेरा बन्ध है, न मोक्ष; भ्रान्ति शान्त और निराश्रय है। विश्व मुझ में स्थित प्रतीत होता है, परन्तु वस्तुतः मुझ में स्थित नहीं। (7) न वेद-शास्त्र, न गुरु, न शिष्य, न देवता - यह आत्मा सदा पूर्ण है, स्वयं ज्योति प्रकाशित है। (8) न मैं कर्ता, न भोक्ता, न कर्म, न देह, न देही - परम ब्रह्म मैं ही हूँ। (12) न राग, न द्वेष, न लोभ, न मत्सर - धर्म-अर्थ-काम-मोक्ष से मुझे क्या प्रयोजन। (14) निर्गुण, निष्क्रिय, शान्त, निर्दोष, निरंजन, आनन्दमय, अद्वन्द्व - यही अवधूत का स्वरूप है।
benefits: Contains the highest Advaita (non-dual) philosophical teachings of Dattatreya
Establishes the identity of Atman and Brahman for spiritual liberation
Frees the mind from bondage, attachment, and duality
Key verses from all chapters for daily contemplation
The ultimate philosophical song of Lord Dattatreya as Avadhuta
description: Dedicated to Lord Dattatreya, the Avadhuta Gita - Key Verses (अवधूत गीता (प्रमुख श्लोक)) is a sacred prayer revered in Hindu tradition. This sacred composition is sourced from Avadhuta Gita of Dattatreya and continues to inspire devotees worldwide. Chanting this stotra with devotion is said to establish the identity of Atman and Brahman for spiritual liberation as well as grant Vairagya (dispassion) and Jnana (transcendental knowledge). Additionally, it is known to free the mind from bondage, attachment, and duality. It is especially recommended on Thursday (Guruvar) and during Guru Purnima and Datta Jayanti for maximum spiritual benefit. Comprising 15 verses, it can be completed in approximately 10 minutes, making it suitable for daily devotional practice. On this page, you can read the complete Avadhuta Gita - Key Verses in Devanagari Sanskrit with English transliteration, Hindi meaning (arth), and free PDF download for offline recitation.

seoFaqs.question: What is the Avadhuta Gita?
seoFaqs.answer: The Avadhuta Gita (अवधूत गीता — Song of the Free Soul) is one of the foremost Advaita Vedanta classics, traditionally attributed to Bhagavan Dattatreya — the combined incarnation of Brahma, Vishnu and Shiva who is regarded as the Adi Guru of Yoga and the source of the Avadhuta tradition of liberated, unattached sages. The text is structured as Dattatreya's teaching to his disciple Skanda (Karttikeya), spanning eight adhyayas and approximately 289 verses (in the standard recension). It expounds in piercingly direct language the absolute non-duality of Atman and Brahman, transcending all dharma-adharma, kriya-akarana, and bondage-liberation distinctions. The opening verse — Ishvara-anugrahad eva pumsam advaita-vasana, Mahad-bhaya-paritranad vipranam upajayate ('Only by the grace of Ishvara does the conviction in non-duality arise in human beings, protecting them from great fear') — is among the most cited Advaita verses. (Source: Avadhuta Gita of Bhagavan Dattatreya.)

seoFaqs.question: Who is an Avadhuta and what does the term mean?
seoFaqs.answer: An Avadhuta is the highest type of realized sage in the Hindu tradition — one who has shaken off (ava-dhu) all bondage of body, mind, ego, social roles, and even the concept of sannyasa itself. The four syllables of A-VA-DHU-TA are traditionally interpreted as: A — akshara (the imperishable Brahman), VA — varenya (the supreme Self), DHU — dhuta-samsara-bandhana (one who has shaken off worldly bondage), TA — tat-tvam-asi (established in That-Thou-Art). An Avadhuta lives beyond all rules of varna-ashrama, may appear as child, madman, or possessed, transcends pleasure-pain, hunger-fullness, friend-foe, and is not recognizable by external marks. Bhagavan Dattatreya is the archetype of the Avadhuta. The Bhagavata Purana (Skandha 11) records Dattatreya's famous discourse to King Yadu on his 24 gurus — illustrating the Avadhuta principle of learning wisdom from every being and circumstance. (Sources: Avadhuta Upanishad; Bhagavata Purana 11.7–9.)

seoFaqs.question: What are the main teachings of the Avadhuta Gita?
seoFaqs.answer: The Avadhuta Gita teaches the most uncompromising form of Advaita Vedanta in eight chapters: (1) the absolute identity of Atman and Brahman — there is no second; (2) Atman is beyond all attributes — neither inside nor outside, neither one nor many, neither pure nor impure; (3) the futility of all rituals, pilgrimages, mantras, asanas, and even meditations once the Self is realized; (4) freedom from all duality — bondage and liberation, knowledge and ignorance, dharma and adharma are themselves illusory; (5) the all-pervasive Cidakasha (consciousness-space) is the only reality; (6) jnana arises by Ishvara-anugraha (divine grace) alone, not by effort; (7) the realized one is beyond praise and blame, action and inaction; and (8) the ultimate teaching that even the words 'Brahman', 'Atman', 'Knowledge', 'Liberation' are themselves mental concepts to be transcended. (Source: Avadhuta Gita 1–8.)

seoFaqs.question: How does the Avadhuta Gita differ from the Bhagavad Gita?
seoFaqs.answer: Both are Gita-genre teachings (Song-style spiritual dialogues) but with different scope and audience. (1) The Bhagavad Gita (700 verses, Mahabharata Bhishma Parva) is delivered by Krishna to Arjuna on the Kurukshetra battlefield — synthesizing Karma Yoga, Bhakti Yoga, Jnana Yoga, and Raja Yoga as complementary paths suitable for householders, warriors, and seekers across temperaments. It accommodates dharma, devotion, and ritual within an Advaita framework. (2) The Avadhuta Gita (289 verses) is Dattatreya's teaching to Skanda — purely Advaita, uncompromising, addressed to the most advanced renunciate seeker. It rejects all duality including the Krishna-Arjuna, devotee-Lord, and seeker-sought distinction. The Bhagavad Gita guides the householder; the Avadhuta Gita confirms the realization of the renunciate. Many sages — including Sri Ramakrishna, Swami Vivekananda, Ramana Maharshi and Nisargadatta Maharaj — held the Avadhuta Gita in particular esteem. (Sources: Bhagavad Gita; Avadhuta Gita; Vivekananda's Complete Works.)

seoFaqs.question: Who was Bhagavan Dattatreya?
seoFaqs.answer: Bhagavan Dattatreya is the unique tri-murti incarnation in Hindu tradition — the combined embodiment of Brahma (the creator), Vishnu (the preserver) and Shiva (the destroyer) — born to the rishi Atri and his ascetic wife Anasuya in the Treta Yuga, when the trinity tested Anasuya's pativrata-shakti. Pleased by her purity, the trinity granted her a son who carried all three of their essences. Dattatreya is regarded as the Adi Guru — the original guru — of yoga, tantra, and Advaita Vedanta, and the source of multiple parampara including the Nath sampradaya, the Avadhuta sampradaya, and the Datta sampradaya widespread in Maharashtra and Karnataka. His major teaching texts include the Avadhuta Gita, the Jivanmukta Gita, the Tripura Rahasya, and his discourses in Bhagavata Purana 11.7–9. He is often depicted with three faces, six arms, four dogs (the four Vedas), and a cow (the earth), with Mount Gandhamadana, Mahur, Girnar, and Audumbar as his principal kshetras. (Sources: Bhagavata Purana 11; Datta Mahatmya; Datta Sampradaya tradition.)

seoFaqs.question: When and how should the Avadhuta Gita be studied for spiritual benefit?
seoFaqs.answer: The Avadhuta Gita is traditionally studied under the guidance of a realized Vedanta acharya, especially during periods of intensive sadhana. Auspicious times include Datta Jayanti (Margashirsha Purnima — Dattatreya's appearance day, falling around November-December), every Thursday (Guruvar — Dattatreya's day as the Adi Guru), the Kartika and Margashirsha months (sacred to Datta sampradaya), and during silent retreat (mauna-vrata). Recommended approach: read 5–10 verses daily with reflection (manana) and contemplation (nididhyasana), preferably after foundational study of Atma Bodha, Tattva Bodha, Vivekachudamani and the Upanishads, since the Avadhuta Gita's terseness assumes prior grounding in Advaita basics. The text is best chanted with a slow, contemplative cadence rather than rapid recitation, allowing each verse to dissolve mental concepts. Key Datta kshetras for parayana include Audumbar, Narsobawadi, Ganagapur, Mahur and Girnar. (Sources: Datta Sampradaya paddhati; Advaita Vedanta acharya tradition.)
